@graph.name: Human Altruism
@graph.description: A framework for understanding giving that starts with the giver — their psychology, identity, and life context — before asking where their resources should go.

@graph.name: Human Altruism
@graph.description: Human Altruism is the practice of giving that starts with understanding who you are — not with calculating where your dollar goes farthest.

@graph.name: Human Altruism
@graph.description: A philanthropic framework that begins with the psychology and identity of the giver rather than the optimization of impact per dollar. Contrasted with Effective Altruism, which prioritizes evidence-based cause selection over donor self-understanding.

acceptedAnswer.text: Human Altruism is the practice of giving that starts with understanding who you are — your psychology, your life context, your reasons — before asking where your resources should go. It holds that generosity is a human act shaped by identity and meaning, not only a mathematical optimization problem.

mainEntity.name: How is Human Altruism different from Effective Altruism?

acceptedAnswer.text: Effective Altruism starts with the cause and asks 'where does my dollar do the most good?' Human Altruism starts with the giver and asks 'why do I want to give, and what kind of giver am I?' Both are rigorous. Human Altruism treats the emotional and psychological dimension of giving as data, not bias to be corrected.

Human Altruism is for anyone who wants to give well but finds that existing frameworks — charity, Effective Altruism, traditional philanthropy — don't match their entry point. That includes first-time donors, people who recently came into wealth, founders, inheritors, people seeking purpose, and those thinking about legacy.

mainEntity.name: Is Human Altruism against Effective Altruism?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. Human Altruism is the missing first chapter. Effective Altruism provides rigorous frameworks for how to give. Human Altruism addresses the prior question: understanding yourself as a giver. Both can coexist and strengthen each other.
